message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier B) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts (but a NEW leaked PII/secret class IS in scope); on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh). Judge ONLY P10-PII-1.

FINDING P10-PII-1 (high): Private LAN/gateway IP + interface + ISP vendor leak in committed transcripts (no IP/network rule)
Location: scripts/capture_transcripts.py (_SECRET_PATTERNS / _scrub — no IP rule); transcripts/sessions/**
Claim: The scrubber covers email/username/name/handle/hostname but has NO rule for IP addresses or network topology. The operator's private LAN IP / gateway (e.g. 192.168.x.y / 10.x / 172.16-31.x), network interface names, and ISP/vendor strings appear in committed transcripts (e.g. from ifconfig/networksetup/route output or the prov namespace context). VERIFY by grepping transcripts/ for RFC1918 patterns (192.168., 10., 172.1[6-9]/2x.) and assessing whether genuine operator network data leaks (not just example IPs in code). Judge whether this is operator-identifying/infra PII in the committed corpus.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the scrubber code and grepping the committed transcripts for RFC1918 IP patterns.

content.text:
This is conclusive. The leak is genuine operator infrastructure data:

- `gateway: [REDACTED]` / `interface: en1` — real default-route output from the operator's LAN
- `[REDACTED]:49206->160.79.104.10:443 (ESTABLISHED)` — the operator's actual private LAN host IP in live `lsof` connection output, paired with real remote endpoints (160.79.104.10 = Anthropic API range)

These are not example IPs in code (the one code-ish hit, `T2_BASE_URL = "http://[REDACTED]:11434"`, is config). The `192.168.50.x` values come from real `route`/`lsof` command output captured in 117 files. The scrubber redacts `/Users/[REDACTED] email, hostnames, etc. (confirmed by `[REDACTED]` appearing right next to the leaked IPs) but has no rule for IP addresses or network topology.

Scope check: The README "honest scope" explicitly discloses "local filesystem paths" and "operator's email" as residual leaks — but says nothing about IP addresses or network topology. So this is a NEW PII class not covered by the documented honest-scope, and it's operator-infra-identifying (private LAN subnet, gateway, host IP, interface name). This matches the in-scope carve-out: "a NEW leaked PII/secret class IS in scope."

input.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the scrubber and grepping the committed corpus.

SCRUBBER: scripts/capture_transcripts.py _SECRET_PATTERNS (lines 40-57) covers sk-ant/GitHub/AWS tokens, bearer, *_TOKEN/_KEY/_SECRET, email, and /Users|/home paths plus dynamic name/handle/hostname rules. There is NO rule for IP addresses or network topology (grep for ip/gateway/interface/192/inet/ifconfig/route in the scrubber returns only docstring/comment text, no regex).

LEAK IS REAL OPERATOR DATA (not example IPs in code): grepping transcripts/ for RFC1918 patterns hits 117 files. Genuine command output includes:
- "---DEFAULT ROUTE---\n    gateway: [REDACTED]\n  interface: en1" (route/networksetup output)
- live lsof: "[REDACTED]:49206->160.79.104.10:443 (ESTABLISHED)" — the operator's actual private LAN host IP ([REDACTED]) and gateway ([REDACTED]), interface en1, paired with real remote Anthropic endpoints (160.79.104.10).
These come from captured ifconfig/route/lsof output, not from code fixtures. The scrubber demonstrably ran (adjacent "/Users/[REDACTED]/" and "[REDACTED]" process owners) yet left all network/IP data intact.

SCOPE: transcripts/README.md "Honest scope" discloses only residual "local filesystem paths" and "operator's email" — it does NOT disclose IP/network-topology leakage. So private LAN subnet/gateway/host-IP/interface is a NEW leaked PII/infra-identifying class, explicitly in scope per the campaign rule ("a NEW leaked PII/secret class IS in scope"). This reveals the operator's home/office LAN addressing ([REDACTED]/24, gateway .1, host .134) across the committed corpus — genuine operator-identifying infra PII. Finding is valid; the scrubber needs an RFC1918/private-IP + interface-name redaction rule and a backfill pass over existing transcripts.

content.text:
Verification complete. Verdict returned: TRUE_POSITIVE.

The finding is genuine. The transcript scrubber in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py` has no IP/network-topology redaction rule, and 117 committed transcript files leak the operator's real private LAN data (gateway [REDACTED], host [REDACTED], interface en1, sourced from captured route/lsof output). This is a new PII class not covered by the documented honest-scope in transcripts/README.md.
